label: vulnerable
description: Direct object reference without ownership check. Any authenticated user can fetch any order by guessing the integer ID. The ORM query is filtered only by primary key, not by the requesting user's identity.
attack_scenario: Attacker logs in, intercepts a GET /orders/42/ request, then iterates order_id from 1 to 10000, exfiltrating all users' order details.

label: vulnerable
description: filter(id=record_id) without tying the result to request.user allows any authenticated user to read medical records belonging to other patients. The None check only prevents 404 errors, not access control bypass.
attack_scenario: Attacker with valid login enumerates /records/{id}, reading other patients' diagnoses, prescriptions, and personal health data.

label: vulnerable
description: JWT is decoded to extract user_id, but user_id is never used to constrain the invoice query. The developer appears to have intended ownership enforcement but implemented it incompletely — classic partial-fix IDOR. Any JWT holder can access any invoice.
attack_scenario: Attacker obtains any valid JWT, sends GET /api/invoice/1 through /api/invoice/50000 iterating all invoices across the entire customer base.

label: vulnerable
description: Both GET and DELETE lack ownership verification. An attacker can not only read arbitrary documents but also delete documents belonging to other users — a destructive IDOR enabling mass data deletion as a denial-of-service attack.
attack_scenario: Attacker iterates DELETE /documents/{id} to wipe all documents in the system, or GET /documents/{id} to exfiltrate sensitive files uploaded by other users.

label: vulnerable
code:
from sqlalchemy.orm import Session

def get_patient_notes(db: Session, note_id: int):
    note = db.query(PatientNote).filter(PatientNote.id == note_id).first()
    return note
description: SQLAlchemy ORM query filters only by primary key. In a FastAPI or Flask context where note_id comes from a URL path parameter, any authenticated user can retrieve clinical notes belonging to any patient. No current_user check is present in the query.
attack_scenario: Healthcare attacker with a valid account iterates note_id to read psychiatric notes, HIV status, or other sensitive records of other patients.

label: vulnerable
code:
def update_user_address(request):
    data = request.json()
    user_id = data['user_id']   # user_id comes from client-supplied JSON body
    new_address = data['address']
    user = User.objects.get(id=user_id)
    user.address = new_address
    user.save()
    return {'status': 'updated'}
description: user_id is read from the POST body rather than from the authenticated session. An attacker can supply any user_id to overwrite another user's address — a write-IDOR (object-level authorisation failure on mutation). This can be exploited to redirect deliveries, intercept packages, or facilitate account takeover.
fix:
def update_user_address(request):
    data = request.json()
    new_address = data['address']
    # Always derive identity from the authenticated session, never from client input
    user = request.user
    user.address = new_address
    user.save()
    return {'status': 'updated'}
attack_scenario: Attacker changes another user's shipping address before a high-value order is delivered, intercepting goods or causing reputational damage.

label: vulnerable
description: LoginRequiredMixin ensures the user is authenticated but does not verify comment ownership. filter(id=comment_id).delete() removes whatever comment has that ID regardless of author. Any logged-in user can delete any comment on the platform.
attack_scenario: Attacker iterates POST /comments/{id}/delete to silence competitor reviews, remove moderation flags, or delete other users' content wholesale.

label: vulnerable
description: get_current_user authenticates the requestor but the query never uses current_user.id to restrict file access. Any authenticated user can download any file by incrementing file_id. The 404-only guard does not constitute access control.
attack_scenario: Attacker downloads all uploaded files across the platform by iterating /files/{id}/download, including confidential contracts, personal photos, and private documents.

label: vulnerable
description: The function accepts employee_id as a parameter without any caller-side ownership validation shown. In an API context where employee_id originates from a URL path or query string, any employee can retrieve any other employee's salary by supplying a different ID. There is no check that the requesting user's ID matches employee_id.
attack_scenario: Employee requests GET /salary/{id} with colleague IDs to compare salaries, violating confidentiality and potentially enabling salary negotiation manipulation or HR disputes.

label: vulnerable
code:
class PrivateMessageView(generics.RetrieveAPIView):
    queryset = PrivateMessage.objects.all()
    serializer_class = PrivateMessageSerializer
    permission_classes = [IsAuthenticated]
description: Using generics.RetrieveAPIView with queryset = PrivateMessage.objects.all() and only IsAuthenticated means any authenticated user can GET /messages/{id} for any message. Django REST Framework's get_object() does perform object-level permission checks only if a custom has_object_permission is defined — which is absent here.
attack_scenario: Attacker iterates GET /messages/{id} to read private messages between other users, enabling stalking, blackmail, or corporate espionage.
